instagram.caption: We just shipped a completely redesigned dashboard for one of our SaaS clients. Clean hierarchy, intuitive navigation, dark mode support. The kind of UI that makes users actually want to log in every morning. Swipe to see the before/after.

linkedin.caption:
Our team just delivered a full dashboard redesign for a SaaS analytics platform. The challenge: make complex data feel approachable without dumbing it down.

Key decisions we made:
→ Sticky left nav with icon-first layout
→ Cards instead of tables for top-level metrics
→ Progressive disclosure for drill-down data
→ Dark theme to reduce cognitive fatigue during long sessions

Result: The client reported a 34% drop in support tickets related to navigation within the first two weeks.

What UI patterns have you found most effective for data-heavy dashboards?

Case study drop: How we redesigned a Shopify storefront and increased checkout conversions by 40% in 90 days.

The problems we found:
• 6-step checkout that felt like filing taxes
• Product images too small on mobile
• No trust signals above the fold
• CTA buttons blending into the background

What we changed:
→ Collapsed checkout to 3 steps with guest option upfront
→ Full-bleed product imagery with zoom on tap
→ Trust badges and reviews placed strategically near CTA
→ High-contrast buttons with micro-animations on hover

Full case study in the comments. Worth a read if you run an agency.

twitter.caption:
We redesigned an e-commerce storefront and saw a 40% conversion lift in 90 days.

The biggest win? Cutting checkout from 6 steps to 3.

Thread on what else we changed 🧵

posts.title: Typography in Brand Systems — Knowledge Post

instagram.caption: Typography is doing 60% of the work in any brand system and it gets half the credit. Here's how we approach type scale, pairing, and hierarchy for every client project. Save this — you'll reference it.

linkedin.caption:
Nobody talks enough about typography in brand systems.

We've audited dozens of brand guidelines over the years and the weakest link is almost always the type system — either too many fonts, no clear hierarchy, or rules that fall apart at small sizes.

Here's what a solid type system actually needs:
→ A primary face for headlines (usually a variable font)
→ A secondary face for body (high x-height, excellent screen rendering)
→ A monospace option for code/data (especially for tech clients)
→ A defined scale (we use a 1.25 modular scale as default)
→ Weight and style rules for emphasis, not just size

The payoff: consistent output across every designer and every touchpoint.
